coredata.dc:title: MgFe2O4-loaded N-doped biochar derived from waste cooked rice for efficient low-temperature desulfurization of H2S
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Yi
Huang, Lijia
Yılmaz, Murat
Zhang, Tian C.
Wang, Yuan
Yuan, Shaojun
coredata.dc:description: To improve their low-temperature desulfurization performance, the activated carbon-based materials have typically been modified by either metal oxide loading or nitrogen doping. However, most of the aforementioned materials are limited to only one of the above modification methods, resulting in relatively poor desulfurization performance. Herein, a novel MgFe2O4-loaded N-doped biochar (MgFe2O4@NBC) was fabricated by one-pot pyrolysis of a nitrogen containing precursor of g-C3N4, cooked rice waste, and metal salts for efficient low-temperature desulfurization of H2S. Different fabrication conditions such as pyrolysis temperature and the nitrogen mixing ratio (i.e., the mass ratio of cooked rice waste to g-C3N4) have been extensively studied. The optimized MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample delivered a superior breakthrough capacity of 1052.83 mg/g in an atmosphere of O2 content of 20% and relative humidity of 80% at 25 °C, and demonstrated high tolerance and stability of desulfurization performance to harsh environmental conditions with 80% relative humidity and no oxygen. The desulfurization mechanism of H2S on the surfaces of the MgFe2O4@NBC composites was attributed to the synergistic effects of reactive adsorption and catalytic oxidation process. The composite also demonstrated excellent reusability, retaining 85% of the initial breakthrough capacity after 5 recycled uses. Therefore, the as-prepared MgFe2O4@NBC composite offers a potentially promising carbon-based desulfurizers for practical applications in the low-temperature desulfurization field.

(a) FT-IR spectra of MgFe2O4@BC-500 and MgFe2O4@NBC-T-2 (T = 400, 500, and 600 °C), (b) XRD patterns of MgFe2O4@NBC-T-2 (T = 400, 450, 500, 550, and 600 °C), Raman spectra of (c) MgFe2O4@NBC-500-X (X = 1, 2, 4) and MgFe2O4@BC-500, and (d) MgFe2O4@NBC-T-2 (T = 400, 500, and 600 °C).

(a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of MgFe2O4@NBC-T-2 (T = 400, 500, and 600 °C) and MgFe2O4@BC-500, (b) pore size distribution calculated from DFT method of MgFe2O4@NBC-T-2 (T = 400, 500, and 600 °C) and MgFe2O4@BC-500.
(a)−(c) SEM images, (d)-(f) TEM images, and (g)-(k) The elemental mapping of C, N, Mg, and Fe elements and the corresponding EDS spectrum of MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2.

XPS of MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2: (a) full-spectra, (b) C 1s core energy level spectra, (c) O 1s core energy level spectra, (d) N 1s core energy level spectra of MgFe2O4@NBC-T-2 (T = 400, 500 and 600 °C), and (e & f) Fe 2p and Mg 1s core energy level spectras.

Breakthrough curves of (a) MgFe2O4@NBC-T-2 (T = 400, 500 and 600 °C) and BC-500, and (b) MgFe2O4@NBC-500-X (X = 1, 2, 4). (c) Breakthrough capacity of adsorbents (n = 3). (d) Regeneration performance of MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 (n = 3). All tests were performed in a relative humidity of 80%, an oxygen content of 20%, an ambient temperature of 25 °C, and a volume space velocity of ca.12000 h−1.

Influence of desulfurization process conditions on MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2: (a) desulfurization temperatures, (b) oxyen content, and (c) relative humidity. (d) Breakthrough capacity under different desulfurization process conditions (n = 3).

XPS of A-MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 (A = after adsorption): (a) full-spectra, (b) C 1s core energy level spectra, (c) N 1s core energy level spectra, (d) Mg 1s core energy level spectra, and (e, f) Fe 2p and S 2p core energy level spectra.

In-situ DRIFTS spectra of H2S reaction on MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 recorded at different conditions: (a) oxygen-free and water-free, (b) oxygen and water-free, (c) oxygen-free and water, and (d) oxygen and water.

head.ce:title: MgFe2O4-loaded N-doped biochar derived from waste cooked rice for efficient low-temperature desulfurization of H2S

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To improve their low-temperature desulfurization performance, the activated carbon-based materials have typically been modified by either metal oxide loading or nitrogen doping. However, most of the aforementioned materials are limited to only one of the above modification methods, resulting in relatively poor desulfurization performance. Herein, a novel MgFe2O4-loaded N-doped biochar (MgFe2O4@NBC) was fabricated by one-pot pyrolysis of a nitrogen containing precursor of g-C3N4, cooked rice waste, and metal salts for efficient low-temperature desulfurization of H2S. Different fabrication conditions such as pyrolysis temperature and the nitrogen mixing ratio (i.e., the mass ratio of cooked rice waste to g-C3N4) have been extensively studied. The optimized MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample delivered a superior breakthrough capacity of 1052.83 mg/g in an atmosphere of O2 content of 20% and relative humidity of 80% at 25 °C, and demonstrated high tolerance and stability of desulfurization performance to harsh environmental conditions with 80% relative humidity and no oxygen. The desulfurization mechanism of H2S on the surfaces of the MgFe2O4@NBC composites was attributed to the synergistic effects of reactive adsorption and catalytic oxidation process. The composite also demonstrated excellent reusability, retaining 85% of the initial breakthrough capacity after 5 recycled uses. Therefore, the as-prepared MgFe2O4@NBC composite offers a potentially promising carbon-based desulfurizers for practical applications in the low-temperature desulfurization field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aerobic composting is a facile and cheap means to deal with the solid organic mixture wastes in urban and rural region. However, the composting process often produces hydrogen sulfide (H2S) gas with a concentration of about 100–500 ppm due to the metabolism of microorganisms [1–3]. H2S is a common toxic and harmful gas pollutant that often poses a threat to equipment, catalysts, pipelines, and even human health [4,5]. Thus, efficient removal of H2S is an urgent need to protect human health and the environment. Many technologies have been developed to address the emission of H2S, including membrane separation [6], photocatalytic degradation [7], bio-desulfurization [8], adsorption [9–11], catalytic oxidation [12,13], etc. Among them, adsorption and catalytic oxidation are considered as the most promising methods for H2S removal at lower concentrations due to their high reactivity, low operation cost, strong removal ability, and mild reaction conditions.

Activated carbon has been widely considered as the most popular adsorbent for low temperature desulphurization due to its abundant pore structure, high specific surface area, and rich surface sites for chemical reactions [14]. General activated carbon materials cannot meet the requirements of industrial desulfurization and often need to be chemically modified. Metal oxides have a strong affinity for H2S and are common desulfurizers in industry [15,16]. However, H2S removal by bare metal oxides is poor. Loading metal oxides on activated carbon not only retains the active phase, but also has developed porosity, thereby significantly improving desulfurization performance. In the recent study, Yang et al. [17] used ZnFe2O4-loaded activated carbon to remove H2S from moist air, increasing its desulfurization capacity from 5.6 to 122.5 mg/g. Chen et al. [5] increased the desulfurization capacity from 214 to 667 mg/g by loading CuFe2O4 on activated carbon. The desulfurization process is a combination of reaction adsorption and catalytic oxidation, in which ZnFe2O4 and CuFe2O4 can react with H2S to realize reaction adsorption, and the surface of the basic carbon material realizes its catalytic oxidation. MgFe2O4, a ferrite with a spinel structure, has been widely used in photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds and adsorption of heavy metal ions [18,19]. Unfortunately, there is very little research on the removal of H2S by MgFe2O4-based desulfurizers despite the fact that its chemical properties are identical to those of ZnFe2O4 and CuFe2O4 [20,21].

Other than metal oxide assisted alteration, N doping is another typical modification technique for room-temperature catalytic H2S oxidation, which can be broken down into two steps: the dissociation of H2S to HS- and then the oxidation of HS- to S [22,23], with the first step being the rate-limiting one [24,25]. One of the best ways to enhance the catalytic oxidation of H2S is through N-doping since it simultaneously raises the amount of basic functional groups and the degree of defects, which can help with H2S adsorption, dissociation, or chemisorption to oxygen (O2). Ma et al. [9] made a biochar adsorbent having a microporous structure and nitrogen-rich functional groups that had an adsorption capacity of H2S (54.8 mg/g) much higher than that of the biochar without adding urea phosphate. Chen et al. [26] prepared nitrogen-enriched cascading porous carbon (NPCS) that exhibited excellent performance for H2S removal (426.2 mg/g) at room temperature. Pyridine-N and pyrrole-N have been proved to be the main functional groups of N-doped porous carbon for boosted H2S adsorption by density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

Metal oxide loading and N-doping are two important means to improve the ability of carbon-based materials to remove H2S. However, to the best of our knowledge, the researchers only focus on one of the modification methods and do not consider combining the two methods to obtain materials with better desulfurization capabilities. An adsorbent material, which combines metal oxide loading and nitrogen doping, can strengthen both the reactive adsorption and catalytic oxidation of H2S, and thus leading to a higher sulfur capacity. In addition to improving the desulfurization performance of the prepared materials, the economic cost of the prepared samples should also be considered. Using waste biomass as raw material to prepare biochar substrates is helpful to reduce costs and expand their industrial application [27].

Accordingly, this study aims to create a new N-doped biochar that is loaded with MgFe2O4 using a one-pot pyrolysis method for high-performance H2S removal. As shown schematically in Fig. 1 , the nitrogen containing precursor of g-C3N4 was first synthesized by the pyrolysis of melamine. Then, the waste cooked rice from the kitchen waste was used as a raw material, which was proved to be a cheap and high-quality carbon source in our previous study [12], and mixed with the g-C3N4 and metal salts (Mg(NO3)2 and FeCl3) to obtain MgFe2O4-loaded N-doped biochar via one-pot pyrolysis method. The Mg(NO3)2 salt provides O and Mg reacts with Fe derived from FeCl3 to form MgFe2O4 particles. g-C3N4 acts as a nitrogen source and is doped on the surface of the carbon material. The success in the synthesis of N-doped biochar loaded with MgFe2O4 was determined by different characterization methods. Desulfurization performance of biochar (such as adsorption capacity and regeneration ability) could be determined by the breakthrough testing of H2S. The influence of desulfurization temperature, relative humidity, and oxygen content on H2S breakthrough ability was systematically determined. Furthermore, the catalytic oxidation and reactive adsorption of H2S by the biochar thus produced at room temperature was systematically studied, and the possible mechanism of H2S removal on the samples was proposed. The combined effects of nitrogen doping and metal oxide loading on H2S elimination were realized for the first time.

Chemical reagents such as melamine (C3H6N6), magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2), and ferric chloride (FeCl3) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals such as hydrochloric acid (HCl), ethanol, and acetone was obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chem. The waste cooked rice was obtained from the food waste in the school cafeteria at Jiangan Campus of Sichuan University. The pretreatment of cooked waste rice was accomplished by washing and filtration technology. The detailed procedures were described in Electronic Supporting Information (ESI) S1.1. Deionized water was used throughout all the experiments, which was manufactured by a reverse osmosis system.

The nitrogen-containing precursor g-C3N4 was prepared by a well-established pyrolysis approach. Typically, a certain amount of melamine was weighed, and placed in a tube furnace (OTF-1200X, HF-Kejing, China) with programmed temperature ramp with a heating rate at 20 °C/min. The pyrolysis reaction was performed in an air atmosphere at 550 °C for 4 h. After the samples cooled naturally, the yellow product was collected and ground into powder with agate to obtain the nitrogen-doped precursor of g-C3N4.

The MgFe2O4@NBC composites were synthesized by a one-pot pyrolysis strategy as shown schematically in Fig. 1, from which the nitrogen-containing precursor (g-C3N4) was used as the nitrogen source, the pretreated cooked rice waste as the carbon source, and metal salts of activators and MgFe2O4 sources. Typically, an aliquot 2 g of the pretreated cooked rice offal, 1.48 g of Mg(NO3)2, 3.24 g of FeCl3 (i.e., Mg and Fe in a 1:2 M ratio) and 1 g of C3N4, were placed in a 150-mL beaker containing 30 mL of deionized water. The mixture was stirred in a water bath with a magnetic stirrer at 25 °C for circa 1.5 h and turned into a yellow, cloudy, viscous liquid. Subsequently, the yellow cloudy viscous liquid was dried at 100 °C for 12 h to remove excessive water. Then, the obtained solid was allowed for carbonization/activation in a programmable temperature tube furnace (OTF-1200X-II, Hefei Kejing Material Technology Co., China) at 500 °C for 2.5 h with a ramp rate of 10 °C·min−1 and a N2 flow rate of 100 mL·min−1. After the pyrolysis and activation treatment, the resultant MgFe2O4-loaded N-doped biochar sample (defined as MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2) was washed with copious amount of deionized water until the filtrate became neutral solution (pH at 7), and then the as-prepared samples were dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h prior to being stored in vacuum desiccator. For comparison purpose, the composite prepared without g-C3N4 addition was named MgFe2O4@BC-500, and was used as control. Moreover, the sample prepared at the carbonization temperature of 500 °C without metal salt activators was named BC-500. Other biochar with different carbonization/activation temperatures and feed-to-nitrogen (cooked rice waste: g-C3N4) ratios was named MgFe2O4@NBC-T-X (T = the carbonization/activation temperature; X = the mass ratio of rice to g-C3N4). The preparation procedures of other samples were described in detail in ESI S1.2–3.

The surface functional groups of the as-prepared composites were investigated by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR, Perkin Elmer Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms at 77 K were obtained on a Micromeritics analyzer ASAP2460 (Micromeritic Instrument Co., Atlanta, GA, USA). Further, specific surface area (SBET) and pore properties were analyzed by combining the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) model, Barret, Joyner and Halenda (BJH) method and Density functional theory (DFT). The surface crystal structure was analyzed on a Rigaku diffractometer (Rigaku Co., Takatsuki, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1541 nm) with a scan range of 10 to 80°, a scan step of 0.02°, and a scan speed of 5°/min. The degree of surface defects of carbon materials was investigated using a DXR Raman microscope (Thermo-Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). The surface morphology and elemental compositions of the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample were analyzed by using a JSM-7610F scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL, Akishima, Tokyo, Japan). Furthermore, the surface composition profiles of the samples were determined by measuring X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra on a Kratos Axis Ultra Spectrometer (Kratos Instrument, Manchester, UK) with a monochrome Al Ka X-ray source (photon 1486.6 eV, pass energy 20.0 eV). The in-situ DRIFTS were performed with a Thermo-Fisher Scientific Is 50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (see the detailed operation process in ESI S1.4). By changing the oxygen content and water content, the infrared spectra of the corresponding absorption peaks on the surface of the material were recorded over time in four cases: oxygen-free and water-free; oxygen and water-free; oxygen-free and water; and oxygen and water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Breakthrough testing of the MgFe2O4@NBC composites
ce:section.ce:para:
The dynamic desulfurization performance of the prepared samples was tested in a fixed bed with a length of 250 mm and an inner diameter of 8 mm. In a glass tube, a 0.2 g sample was put before being pre-humidified in humid nitrogen for an hour. A stream of N2 containing 500 ppm H2S and 0–30% O2 with the relative humidity of 0–80% was then passed through the sample column (the quartz tube) at a total flow rate of 100 mL/min. The H2S concentration was determined by a gas chromatograph (GC2060, Shanghai Ruimin GC Instruments Inc., China) equipped with a flame photometric detector (FPD). The details of the fixed-bed dynamic desulfurization device were schematically shown in ESI Fig. S1. The desulfurization temperature was controlled by a constant temperature water tank at 15–35 °C. The H2S in the exhaust gas was neutralized with a 1 mol/L NaOH solution. When Ct/C0 reached at 90%, the breakthrough test was ceased. The breakthrough capacity (mg/g) of the samples was calculated by Eq. (1)

where Q was the total flow rate of the feed gas (mL/min), MW was the molecular mass of H2S (34 g/mol), C0 was the H2S concentrations in the inlet, respectively. t was the breakthrough time (min), m was the mass of samples (g), Ct was H2S concentration at the outlet, which varied with breakthrough time, and VL was gas molar volume (=about 24.5 L/mol at 25 °C and 1.01 × 105 Pa). The experiment was repeated thrice for each sample to assess the reliability of the collected data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regeneration and reuse of the MgFe2O4@NBC composite
ce:section.ce:para: After the dynamic breakthrough testing, the exhausted MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample (named as A-MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2) was pyrolytically regenerated at 500 °C for 2.5 h in a tube furnace with a nitrogen flow rate at 100 mL/min and a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The pyrolyzed sample was washed with deionized water and filtered until the pH of the filtrate became about 7, and vacuum-dried at 60 °C to obtain a regenerated sample, which was used in the next breakthrough test. Five desulfurization/regeneration cycle tests were performed.

Fig. 2 a shows that all the samples have a strong vibrational peak with wavenumber at 3419 cm−1, corresponding to surface water OH stretching [28,29]. The vibrational peak at 1617 cm−1 may be attributed to the in-plane stretching vibration of sp2 hybrid carbons [30,31]. The vibrational peaks observed at 1401 and 1105 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of CN and COC[32]. Comparing the characteristic peaks of MgFe2O4@BC-500 without nitrogen introduction and MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2, the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample shows a stronger characteristic CN peak signal, indicating the successful doping of N. The subsequent XPS characterization further illustrates the successful doping of N. With the increase in carbonization/activation temperature, each characteristic peak has some decrease, but from the infrared spectrum of MgFe2O4@NBC-600–2, there are still abundant chemical bonds observed on its surface.

The crystallographic structures of the as-prepared samples were characterized by XRD analysis and are shown in Fig. 2b. In light of the characteristic peak of MgFe2O4@NBC-600–2, the active phase is of a spinel MgFe2O4 phase (PDF#17–0464), and no other impurity peak can be observed, indicative of the successful loading of MgFe2O4. The peaks at 18.3°, 30.2°, 35.5°, 37.2°, 43.2°, 53.6°, 57.1°, 62.7°, correspond to (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of MgFe2O4, respectively. When the activation temperature is lower than 600 °C, the diffraction peak intensity of MgFe2O4 is very low, and some diffraction peaks cannot be clearly resolved. This result may be ascribed to the fact that the crystallinity degree of MgFe2O4 is too low to be detected by XRD analyses, or that the MgFe2O4 particles are well dispersed on the NBC substrates. The increase in the dispersity of MgFe2O4 is favorable to form more active sites on the composite materials, and thus leading to the improved activity and stability of the adsorbents [33]. At carbonization/activation temperature of 400 °C, MgFe2O4 is already formed on the surface. Overall, with increasing carbonization/activation temperature, the intensity of the diffraction peak of MgFe2O4 becomes stronger, which is associated with the grain size of MgFe2O4 itself and the properties of the supported biomass carbon carrier [34]. With increasing carbonization/activation temperature, the specific surface area and pore structure of the biochar undergo a noticeable change, thus affecting the relative intensity of the diffraction peak of the MgFe2O4 crystals, which is confirmed by the subsequent BET results.

The degree of structure defect on the surface of the as-synthesized samples were investigated by Raman characterization. Fig. 2c and d show that the two characteristic peaks at shifts of 1350 and 1590 cm−1 correspond to D band and G band, respectively[35]. The D band originates from the defective and disordered carbon, while the G band is attributed to the crystallized graphitic sp2 carbon [36,37]. The D/G band intensity ratio (ID /IG ) characterizes the degree of structural defects of carbon materials [38]. The ID/IG values of MgFe2O4@NBC-500, MgFe2O4@NBC-500–4, MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2, and MgFe2O4@NBC-500–1 were 0.74, 0.84, 0.92, and 1.04, respectively. With increasing the mass ratio of g-C3N4 to raw material (waste cooked rice), the structural defect of the material surface is observed to increase. The carbonization/activation temperature also has an important effect on the defect degree of carbon materials, and the defect degree of the prepared materials reaches the maximum when the carbonization/activation temperature is 500 °C (See Fig. 2d). The ID /IG values of various samples with nitrogen content and carbonization/activation temperature are found to be around 0.73–1.04, confirming the presence of abundant defect structure. The abundance of carbon defects can promote the chemisorption of O2 and activates the chemisorbed O2 to O2*, thereby accelerating the catalysis from HS- to S element [39,40].

The activity of metal oxides is often affected by the specific surface area and pore size of the support. In the carbonization/activation process of carbon materials, temperature is often the main factor affecting the pore structure and degree of carbonization. Therefore, the nitrogen adsorption–desorption method is used to determine the BET specific surface area and pore size distribution of the samples at different carbonization/activation temperatures as shown in Fig. 3 and Table 1 . According to the IUPAC classification, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of all samples were of Type I with a weak H4-type hysteresis loop, indicating the hierarchical pore structure of the as-synthesized samples [41]. Upon increasing the carbonization/activation temperature, the initial increase and then decrease in the specific surface and total pore volume are observed for the as-synthesized sample (Table 1). From the pore size distribution in Fig. 3b, it can be seen that the pore size of the sample exhibits a microporous and mesoporous structure ranging from 1 to 5 nm. When the carbonization/activation temperature is 500 °C, the specific surface area and the pore volume are at maximum of 222.91 m2/g and 0.31 cm3/g, respectively. As the carbonization/activation temperature increases, the degree of carbonization of the material increases, and the specific surface area and pore volume increase. Generally, a large surface area allows for better dispersion of small sized nano-metals, which is further confirmed in the subsequent TEM characterization. When the carbonization/activation temperature is increased to 600 °C, MgFe2O4 particles increase and deposit on the surface and pores of carbon materials, leading to a corresponding reduction in specific surface area and pore volume, which is well consistent with the previous XRD results. The as-prepared carbon materials are mainly mesoporous, and such a mesoporous structure is often more conducive to the storage of desulfurization products.

The morphology and chemical composition of the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample was further studied by high-resolution TEM, SEM and EDS mapping. As can be seen from Fig. 4 a-4c, the as-synthesized samples have rich porous structures and morphologically complex hierarchical structures. The three-dimensional (3D) porous channel structure is conductive to diffusion of gas and products, and is easy for the deposition of desulfurization products. Fig. 4f shows the high-resolution TEM image of sample MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2, the lattice spacing is observed to be circa 0.252 nm, and the exposed crystal plane is MgFe2O4 (311), which is consistent with the XRD characterization results. Low resolution TEM results (Fig. 4d-4e) show no obvious metal oxide agglomerations on the surface of the material. EDS mapping (Fig. 4g-k) of the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample shows that the elements are uniformly dispersed on the surface of the material, consistent with the low-resolution TEM results. In addition, the surface N content reaches 5.36%, and the atomic ratio of Mg to Fe is close to 1:2, which corresponds to the theoretical value of MgFe2O4 chemical formula. Taken together, these results show that the metal oxide MgFe2O4 has been successfully loaded onto the surface of the material and a certain amount of nitrogen is doped onto the surface.

In order to seek deep understanding into the surface chemical composition of the as-prepared materials, XPS characterization on the samples were performed. Fig. 5 and Table 2 present the XPS spectra of the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample. The wide scan XPS spectrum of MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 (Fig. 5a) shows the presence of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, Mg 1s and Fe 2p signals on the surface of the material. Table 2 shows the chemical compositions of the sample surfaces prepared at different activation temperatures. The atomic ratio of Fe to Mg on the surface of all samples is 2, which corresponds to the theoretical value of MgFe2O4 formula. As expected, the nitrogen content at the surface of the samples decreases with increasing carbonization/activation temperature. It is interesting to note that all the samples have a high oxygen content, and the sample at a carbonization/activation temperature of 500 °C has the highest oxygen content up to 32.56%.

Fig. 5b shows the deconvoluted C 1s core level spectrum of the sample MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2, with five characteristic peaks with binding energies (BEs) at 284.7, 285.9, 287.3, 288.7 and 290.0 eV, corresponding to CC, CN, CO, O-CO and π-π* species, respectively [42,43]. The CN bond at 285.9 eV and O-CO bond at 288.7 eV are consistent with the FTIR characterization results, further confirming the successful doping of N. The surface of the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample is rich in oxygen (32.56%, as shown in Table 2), and its oxygen exists in various forms. Fig. 5c show the curve-fitted O 1s core-level spectrum. The three peaks with BEs at 533.5, 532.5 and 531.2 eV contribute to CO, OC-O and CO bonds, respectively, which corresponds to CO bonds in the deconvoluted C 1s core-level spectrum [25,44]. The peak with BE at 530.1 eV is lattice oxygen, which may be related to the regular tetrahedral and regular octahedral structures in MgFe2O4. The N groups of carbon-based materials are of great importance in a catalytic oxidation reaction. Considering its heterocyclic structure, pyridine-N has been extensively recognized as the main active sites of selective catalytic oxidation of H2S on the surface of carbon-based catalysts [25]. Hence, by further partitioning the distribution of different N species, N 1s can be curve-fitted into four peak components with BEs at 398.1, 400, 401.5 and 402.8 eV, corresponding to pyridine-N, pyrrole-N, graphite-N and oxidized N, respectively [45,46] (Fig. 5d). Table 2 shows that the total nitrogen content of the samples decreases with the increase in the carbonization/activation temperature. When the activation temperature is 500 °C, the content of pyridine nitrogen is the highest.

Fig. 5e displays the deconvoluted Fe 2p core-level XPS spectrum. Two peak contributions with BEs at 710.5 eV and 712.7 eV (2p 3/2) is assignable to the Fe3+ in an octahedral site and a tetrahedral site of MgFe2O4, respectively [47,48].The characteristic peak with BE at 719.1 eV is attributable to a satellite peak, indicating that the valence state of Fe in MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 is Fe3+. The contribution of the deconvoluted Mg 1s (Fig. 5f) with BE at 1303.6 eV indicates the presence of Mg2+. These above XPS analyzes once again confirm that MgFe2O4 crystals have been successfully loaded on the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the H2S removal performance on MgFe2O4@NBC composite
ce:section.ce:para: The desulfurization performance of each sample was tested in a fixed bed reactor with a desulfurization temperature of 25 °C. Apart from H2S, no other gases containing sulfur can be detected in the desulfurization test. Fig. 6 a and b show the H2S breakthrough curves of the as-fabricated samples at different carbonization/activation temperatures and nitrogen doping ratios. The breakthrough capacity of H2S on the BC-500 sample, which was produced in the absence of an activator and nitrogen doping, is only circa 15.49 mg/g (Fig. 6c). This result is due to the underdeveloped pore structure of sample BC-500 and the presence of insufficient oxidative sites of H2S, because, typically, the activator has an important effect on the development of the pore structure and the loading of metal oxides, while the N source is directly related to the number of nitrogenous active sites [49].

The desulfurization ability of the prepared samples is significantly improved by the addition of activator and N source. Fig. 6a shows that the H2S removal ability of the modified sample is significantly improved, while the carbonization/activation temperature has a large impact on its desulfurization ability. When the activation temperature is 400 °C, the H2S breakthrough capacity of the MgFe2O4@NBC-400–2 sample is 527.98 mg/g. A desulfurization ability of 1052.83 mg/g can be reached when the carbonization/activation temperature is 500 °C. As the activation temperature continues to rise to 600 °C, the desulfurization performance of the as-prepared sample decreases to 798.67 mg/g. This may be associated with the lower specific area of the sample obtained at 600 °C. In contrast, the as-prepared sample obtained at 500 °C has the largest specific surface area (222.91 m2/g) and mesoporous volume (0.31 cm3/g), which is conducive to the storage of sulfur products. After determining the optimum a carbonization/activation temperature, the effect of nitrogen ratio on desulfurization performance was investigated. As the ratio of raw material to N source increases, the breakthrough capacity of MgFe2O4@NBC-500-X first increases and then decreases. When the ratio of the raw material to N source is 2:1, the as-prepared MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 desulfurizer has the best breakthrough ability, which can reach up to 1052.82 mg/g. It can be seen that the breakthrough capacity of H2S on the as-prepared composite sample is substantially improved from 15.49 mg/g to 1052.83 mg/g by optimizing the activation rate and the ratio of raw material to nitrogen source.

Metal oxide loading and N doping are the most common room temperature modification methods of carbon-based desulfurizers. Table 3 lists the desulfurization performance of either metal oxide-loaded or nitrogen-doped carbon-based materials reported previously in literatures. The desulfurizer MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 thus prepared successfully carries MgFe2O4 on the surface and is doped with a large amount of pyridine-N, resulting in its breakthrough capacity (1052.83 mg/g) much higher than most values previously reported in studies about metal oxide loading or N doping. MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 combines the modification methods of metal oxide loading and nitrogen doping for the first time and achieves an extremely high desulfurization ability.

The regeneration ability of a desulfurizer is a key index to assess its applicability for commercial applications. Although the sample of YC-0.5–2 (7) shows the highest desulfurization capacity (Table 3), it has very poor regeneration performance [49], which limits its industrial application. The exhausted MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 name as A-MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 is regenerated by thermal treatment. Fig. 6d shows that after 5 cycles of thermal regeneration, the regeneration efficiency of MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 is excellent, still remaining above 85% of the original desulfurization capacity. Thus, this novel composite should have important industrial application prospects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of desulfurization conditions on H2S removal performance
ce:section.ce:para: For practical application, the desulfurization performance and operation cost of the desulfurizer are significantly concerned with the desulfurization operation conditions. Therefore, in order to further investigate its potential as a desulfurizer in practical industrial applications, the influence of different reaction parameters (including reaction temperature, oxygen content, relative humidity) on the performance/characteristics of the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 composites was comprehensively studied.

Fig. 7 a shows the breakthrough curves of sample MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 at different desulfurization temperatures and its corresponding desulfurization capacity is shown in Fig. 7d. At a desulfurization temperature of 15 °C, a H2S removal capacity of 1180 mg/g can be obtained. As the reaction temperature is increased to 25 °C, the desulfurization capacity slightly decreases to circa1052.83 mg/g. When the reaction temperature increases from 25 °C to 35 °C, the desulfurization ability of the sample decreases significantly to 420.55 mg/g, indicating the as-prepared sample is deactivated in a short time. These results indicate that the material is suitable for desulfurization at a lower temperature of 15 °C with the best desulfurization ability at 1180 mg/g.

In order to examine in-depth effects of oxygen content on the desulfurization process, this study performed desulfurization tests at the oxygen content of 0%, 10%, 20%, and 30%. Fig. 7b and d show that, in the absence of oxygen, the sample still shows a good breakthrough capacity of 580.64 mg/g, possibly ascribed to the rich oxygen atoms in the material itself as confirmed by XPS results. These dissolved oxygen in the water film on the surface of the as-prepared samples reacts with the H2S in the reaction desulfurization process. As the oxygen content increases, the sample shows a stronger desulfurization ability, and the best desulfurization effect is 1052.83 mg/g when the oxygen content is 20%. However, if the oxygen content increases further to 30%, the desulfurization capacity undergoes a significant decrease with the breakthrough capacity being only 690.57 mg/g. This may be caused by the competitive adsorption between O2 and H2S under extremely abundant oxygen conditions, so H2S is difficult to be captured by the as-prepared biochar materials. Particularly, the active adsorptive sites on the sample surface might be completely occupied by O2 under a high O2 atmosphere condition. Therefore, the as-prepared biochar materials can accomplish the optimum desulfurization performance in an air-like atmosphere with a medium to low oxygen content.

In practical application, H2S gas is usually accompanied by a certain water content. Thus, it is of great application value to explore the desulfurization ability of the as-prepared samples under different humidity. An adequate amount of H2O is believed to act as a water film covering the surface of carbon materials, which is helpful for the dissociation of H2S molecules into HS- and promotes the catalytic oxidation process [38,50]. Interestingly, water plays a significant role in transferring the elemental sulfur product by being acted as a conveyor belt from porous channel of the carbon-based catalyst to the outer surface of catalyst, however, the coverage of excessive water on the active sites inevitably results in the inhibition of the product desorption from the catalyst surface [24]. Therefore, the relative humidity of 0%, 40% and 80% was chosen to investigate the humidity effect (Fig. 7c). Under anhydrous conditions, the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample rapidly loses its desulfurization ability and its breakthrough capacity is only 370.85 mg/g (Fig. 7d). The breakthrough capacity of MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 experiences a significant increase as the relative humidity increased from 0 to 80%. Such results clearly demonstrate the high tolerance of the desulfurizer MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 to steam concentrations in the process feed. To sum up, these results of all desulfurization condition factors confirm the potential for practical application of MgFe2O4@NBC desulfurizer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The postulated mechanism of H2S removal on the MgFe2O4@NBC composite
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the desulfurization mechanism of the MgFe2O4@NBC composite, the exhausted sample A-MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 was analyzed after the dynamic test to examine the oxidation products. The distribution of elements on its surface was tested with EDS mapping. Fig. S2 shows that a large number of sulfur species appear in the desulfurized sample. The distribution of these sulfur species on the carbon nanosheets is relatively uniform (Fig. S2). To obtain further qualitative and quantitative information about the products, an XPS measurement was performed on the exhausted desulfurizer, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8a shows the survey scan XPS spectrum of the A-MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample. The S 2p signal appears at the binding energy (BE) position of 160–170 eV, but does not appear in the fresh MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 desulfurizer (Fig. 5a), indicating that a catalytic oxidation reaction occurs in the desulfurization process, and sulfur-containing species are generated, which are well consistent with the EDS spectra results shown in Fig. S2. Furthermore, the deconvoluted S 2p core-level spectrum (Fig. 8f) shows that the orbit-spin doublet peaks of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 are located at 163.7 and 165.1 eV, respectively [51], corresponding to elemental sulfur, whilst the peak components with BEs at 168.6 eV and 169.8 eV are attributable to sulfates [45,52], indicating that elemental sulfur is over-oxidized. The small peak with BE at 161.8 eV corresponds to the metal sulfides [53]. The corresponding contributions from the sulfur species of SO4 2-, S, and MSx in A-MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2, as determined from the corresponding spectral area ratio, are 25.6%, 72.3%, and 2.1%, respectively. In particular, no sulfur-containing gases such as SO2 and SO3 are detected during the pre-breakthrough dynamic desulfurization test, suggesting that all sulfur products are stored in the exhausted desulfurizer. The product is mainly sulfur element. It has previously been reported that the edges of carbon catalysts, which are rich in structural defects, can activate O2 to form oxygen radicals. Raman results (Fig. 2c) show that N-doping greatly increases the defect degree of the sample, allowing oxygen to be activated to form oxygen free radicals on the surface of the material.

In order to deepen the understanding of the desulfurization process and further explore the desulfurization mechanism, in-situ DRIFTS experiments were performed under four conditions of oxygen-free and water-free, oxygen and water-free, oxygen-free and water, as well as oxygen and water. Fig. 9 a shows the in-situ DRIFTS of the sample MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 in the dynamic desulfurization test in the absence of oxygen and water. The band around 3650 cm−1 is ascribed to the interaction between hydroxyl group and H2S [54]. The band at 1620 cm−1 is attributable to the H-OH bending vibration of H2O [55]. It should be noted that under oxygen-free and water-free conditions, there is only a small signal value at 1620 cm−1 and no significant change with time, which means that oxygen and water play an important role in the desulfurization process. This is consistent with the aforementioned results of the desulfurization conditions (desulfurization capacity of only 370.85 mg/g in the absence of water and oxygen). Other in situ DRIFTS bands appear in the wavenumber region from 790 to 1200 cm−1, i.e., at 790, 970, 1050, and 1130 cm−1, which are characteristic peaks corresponding to different sulfur species [56,57]. Under oxygen-free and water-free conditions, only weak sulfuric acid signal levels occur at 1050 and 970 cm−1, which is well consistent with our previous test results [14]. The in-situ DRIFTS under oxygen and water-free conditions are shown in Fig. 9b. As we can see, the peaks of the sulfur-containing species are significantly increased, indicating that the presence of oxygen significantly increases the progress of the catalytic oxidation process. At the same time, the characteristic peak of adsorbed water on the surface keeps increasing, which may be the chemical reaction among MgFe2O4, H2S and O2 to generate water. This is confirmed by the XPS results from Mg 1s. Comparing the spectra of Mg 1s before and after desulfurization (Fig. 8d), the peak value of the sample after desulfurization has an obvious deviation, and the binding energy has a deviation of about 0.2 eV, which is related to the electronegativity of non-metallic elements combined with Mg2+. The electronegativity of O is higher than that of S. Mg2+ is mainly present in the form of Mg-O in the sample before desulfurization, while the electronegativity is balanced after desulfurization. Considering that there is no other non-metallic element that can form compounds with Mg, Mg-S is generated. XPS spectra of Fe 2p confirm the formation of Fe-O-Fe after detachment (Fig. 8e).

Fig. 9c shows the in-situ DRIFTS of MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 in the presence of water and the absence of oxygen. At the beginning of the reaction, the characteristic peak of the adsorbed water on the surface continues to rise and accumulate (with wavenumber at 1620 cm−1), and after 30 min begins to decrease. This is because at the beginning of the reaction, water gas accumulates continuously on the material surface and forms a water film. After 30 min, the adsorbed water on the surface is saturated and no longer increases. The weakly dissociated O in the water film and the O of the material itself react with H2S to generate sulfur elements. Sulfur elements continuously accumulate as the reaction progresses. When the accumulation reaches a certain amount, sulfur elements start to be oxidized to SO4 2-, corresponding to the characteristic peaks of 970 and 1130 cm−1. The characteristic peaks do not change significantly after 300 mins, indicating that the reaction is stopped and the material has been inactivated. Fig. 9d shows the in-situ DRIFTS spectrum under oxygen and water conditions. In the presence of both oxygen and water, the sample desulfurization time is the longest and the material has still not been deactivated after 360 mins. It is found that the desulfurization effect is the best under this condition, which is in good agreement with the previous performance test.

Based on the above characteristic results and the most commonly accepted catalytic H2S oxidation process (the vapor–liquid-solid mechanism), the following MgFe2O4@NBC desulfurization mechanism can be proposed (Fig. 10 ) [23,58]: i) water film formation (Eq. 2 below) and adsorbed H2S. When the raw gas reaches the surface of the desulfurizer with a certain humidity, a film of water forms on its surface. Due to the influence of the N-doping on the material surface, an alkaline water film forms and H2S is adsorbed on the alkaline water film. ii) Dissociation of H2S (Eq. 3) and activation of oxygen (Eq. 4). The dissociation of H2S is the first and key step in its catalytic oxidation. H2S in the raw gas dissolves in the alkaline water film formed on the surface and dissociates into HS-, and the free HS- is randomly dispersed in each active site of the carbon material. The adsorbed O2 is activated to O2* on the carbon surface. iii) HS− oxidation. The active O2* oxidizes HS- to produce elemental sulfur, which deposits on the carbon surface and pore structure (Eq. 5). iv) The adsorbed H2S and O2 react with MgFe2O4 in the water film to form FeOOH and MgS (Eq. 7). The desulfurization process is a combination of catalytic oxidation and reactive adsorption. The corresponding description is also stated in Eqs. (2)-(7):
(2) H2Ogas → H2Oliq
(3) H2S → HS- + H+
(4) O2 → O2*
(5) 2HS- + O2*→ 2S + 2OH–
(6) OH– + H+→ H2O
(7) MgFe2O4 + 4H2S + 3/2 O2 → MgS + 2FeOOH + 3H2O + 3S

In order to better understand the proportion of reactive adsorption and catalytic oxidation in H2S removal process, the mass loading of MgFe2O4 in MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample is calculated to be 32.4% by using TGA characterization (Fig. S3). The stoichiometric reaction capacity of the adsorbent is calculated to be 44.1 mg/g according to the reaction Eq. 7 [59]. The H2S removal capacity of the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample is 1052.83 mg/g (Fig. 6). Therefore, the proportions of reactive adsorption and catalytic oxidation can be calculated as 4.1% and 95.9%, respectively. This result further confirms the outstanding oxidation capability of such MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample. BET analysis of A-MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 shows that the specific surface area decreases from 222.91 to 108.22 m2/g. Meanwhile, the total pore volume decreases from 0.31 to 0.09 cm3 /g. Mesoporous volumes range from 0.24 to 0.04 cm3/g (Table S1, Fig. S4). It is interesting to note that almost all reduced pore sizes are mesoporous, indicating that the sulfur compounds generated are mainly present in mesoporous pores. In combination with the XPS results, the desulfurization products are mainly elemental S, indicating that the reduction in specific surface area and pore volume is possibly ascribed to the coverage of the elemental S product on the desulfurizer surfaces. Sulfur products cover the active part and the alkaline part, and thus leading to gradual inactivation of the sample.

In this study, a novel MgFe2O4-loaded N-doped biochar (MgFe2O4@NBC) was successfully prepared via a one-pot pyrolysis method by using CN as a nitrogen source, waste cooked rice as a carbon source, and metal salts of Mg(NO3)2 and FeCl3 as metal oxide precursors and activators (pore forming agents), and the catalytic behavior of the novel composites as desulfurizers for the room-temperature oxidation of H2S has been studied in detail. The XRD and TEM results confirmed that MgFe2O4 was successfully loaded onto the surface of carbon materials with a relatively uniform dispersion. XPS results show that the surface of the sample is rich in nitrogen after nitrogen doping modification. The doped N atom and the supported metal oxides have a strong promoting effect on the generation of active oxygen radicals and the adsorption of H2S, respectively, whereby the H2S conversion rate reaches 100% at the initial stage of the reaction with high catalytic activity. In addition, the materials prepared are mainly mesoporous at about 4 nm, which is more conducive to storage and transfer of sulfur products. By analyzing the elemental composition changes on the surface of samples during and after desulfurization, the desulfurization process is a combination of catalytic oxidation and reactive adsorption. Benefitting from these advantages, the MgFe2O4@NBC-500–2 sample provided excellent H2S removal with a breakthrough capacity of up to 1052.83 mg/g at 25 °C. Furthermore, the as-fabricated MgFe2O4@NBC desulfurizer has a high regeneration capacity and can retain 85% of the initial breakthrough capacity after 5 regeneration cycles, which is crucial for industrial applications. This study provides a new strategy for developing a novel low-temperature and cost-effective biochar-based desulfurizer.
